intro: Philemon is a single chapter, but it rewards slow, communal reading. These three sessions move a group through the letter’s three movements — the warm preface, the heart of the appeal, and its costly climax and close. Each runs from an opening question through observation and interpretation to application and prayer. Have the letter open; read the assigned verses aloud together before discussing.

sessions.title: Grace That Prepares the Ask
sessions.big_idea: Before Paul asks anything, he thanks God for Philemon and praises his love — winning the heart before the appeal. Grace is the atmosphere in which hard requests can be carried.
sessions.open: When someone needs to ask you for something difficult, what makes you more — or less — willing to say yes?
observe: How does Paul describe himself in v.1? What does he call Philemon?
Who else is included in the greeting (v.2)? Why might Paul address a whole household for a private matter?
What two things has Paul “heard” about Philemon (v.5)?
interpret: Paul calls himself “a prisoner of Christ Jesus” rather than “an apostle.” What does that choice do to the tone of the letter?
What is Paul actually praying for in v.6 — and how might it connect to the request still to come?
What does it mean that Philemon has “refreshed the hearts of the saints” (v.7)?
apply: Where do you tend to lead with authority when love would move a person further?
Whose love has “refreshed” you? How could you be that person for someone this week?
sessions.pray: Thank God for people whose love has refreshed you; ask for grace to prepare your own hard conversations with genuine gratitude rather than pressure.
sessions.leader_notes: The point of vv.1–7 is Paul’s method: praise and prayer before petition. Draw the group to notice that “prisoner, not apostle” (v.1) sets affection over authority — the key to the whole letter. On v.6 (a famously knotty verse), keep it simple: Paul prays that Philemon’s faith, shared and active, would bear visible fruit in love — exactly the love the appeal for Onesimus will require. Note that “refresh” in v.7 returns in v.20: the man who refreshed many is asked to refresh Paul by receiving one more.

sessions.title: Love’s Appeal — No Longer a Slave
sessions.big_idea: Paul lays down the right to command and instead beseeches — and names Onesimus not as a runaway but as “my child.” The gospel remakes the master–slave bond into brotherhood.
sessions.open: Have you ever had a relationship change categories — someone who was a rival, employee, or stranger becoming a friend or family? What shifted?
observe: What could Paul do “boldly,” and what does he do instead (vv.8–9)?
How does Paul describe Onesimus in vv.10–11? What was he “before,” and what is he “now”?
What does Paul say Onesimus is to become to Philemon in v.16?
interpret: Why does Paul refuse to command, even though he could? What does his restraint accomplish that a command could not?
Onesimus’s name means “useful.” How does Paul play on that in v.11, and what does grace do to a “useless” life?
“No longer as a slave, but... a beloved brother” (v.16) — how does this reframe the whole relationship without abolishing it by decree?
apply: Who in your life do you relate to mainly by role or transaction, when Christ would have you see a brother or sister?
Where might God be asking you to receive back someone who wronged you?
sessions.pray: Ask God to show you anyone you have reduced to a role or a grievance, and for grace to receive them as family in Christ.
sessions.leader_notes: The engine of this section is Paul’s deliberate refusal to command (vv.8–9): a freely given kindness is worth more than an extorted one. Help the group feel the tact — the name “Onesimus” is withheld until v.10, after Paul has called him “my child.” On v.16: Paul does not (yet) demand emancipation, but calling a slave a “beloved brother” is a truth the institution cannot survive — F.F. Bruce’s image is of an atmosphere in which slavery could only wither. Keep application honest: this passage presses on our own transactional relationships and unforgiven wrongs.

sessions.title: Charge It to My Account
sessions.big_idea: Paul reaches his climax: receive Onesimus as you would receive me, and charge any debt to my account. It is the gospel of substitution in a personal register — and it ends, as it began, in grace.
sessions.open: Has anyone ever covered a debt or taken a hit for you? What did it cost them, and how did it change things?
observe: On what basis does Paul ask Philemon to receive Onesimus (v.17)?
What does Paul offer to do about any wrong or debt (vv.18–19)? What does he remind Philemon he owes?
What does Paul expect and hope for in vv.21–22?
interpret: “Receive him as you would receive me” (v.17). What does it mean that Paul so identifies himself with Onesimus?
How is “charge it to my account; I will repay” (vv.18–19) a picture of what Christ does for us?
Paul says he knows Philemon will “do even more than I say” (v.21). What might the unspoken “more” be — and why leave it unspoken?
apply: Where is God asking you to absorb a cost so that someone else can be reconciled?
The letter ends in grace over a whole household. What relationship in your community needs that atmosphere of grace?
sessions.pray: Thank Christ for standing surety for your debt; ask for courage to bear a cost this week so that someone else might be received back.
sessions.leader_notes: v.17–19 is the theological summit: Paul’s identification (“receive him as myself”) and substitution (“charge it to me; I will repay”) together mirror the gospel — many of the witnesses hear an echo of Christ bearing the believer’s debt. Note the gentle irony of v.19: Philemon owes Paul “your own self.” On v.21, the “more than I say” is widely read as a hint at freedom — implied, never demanded, leaving the decision Philemon’s own. Close by reading v.25 together: the letter begins and ends in grace.
